question_title: Will the June 30, 2026 hearing in U.S. v. Maduro Moros (SDNY) be rescheduled before May 31, 2026?
summary: The June 30, 2026 hearing was set just 16 days ago (May 4) by mutual agreement after a joint letter from both parties. The case is in a quiet pretrial phase with no pending motions, no active disputes, and time excluded until June 30. Only 11 days remain before the May 31 resolution deadline. For YES, an unexpected development would be needed: surprise motions, plea deal, judicial health emergency, or diplomatic intervention. No visible precursors exist for any of these. The prior rescheduling (mid-March to March 26) was driven by the then-active fees dispute, now resolved. While tail risks exist given the case's extraordinary profile and Judge Hellerstein's age (92), the overwhelming probability is that the hearing remains as scheduled through May 31. Estimate: 13% YES.

factors.description: Hearing was set very recently (May 4, 2026) by mutual agreement of both parties via joint letter - strong signal of scheduling stability
Only 11-day window (May 20-31) for rescheduling to occur - very short timeframe limits probability of any change
Case is in quiet pretrial phase - no pending motions, OFAC fees dispute resolved, defense motions withdrawn without prejudice, time excluded until June 30
Prior rescheduling has occurred in this case (mid-March to March 26), establishing that schedule changes are not unprecedented in this matter
Extraordinarily complex and high-profile case with multiple dimensions (Argentina extradition, SAMs, fugitive co-defendants, diplomatic pressures) creating more channels for unexpected developments
Judge Hellerstein is 92 years old - health-related scheduling disruptions are possible though unlikely in just 11 days
No known catalysts visible: no recent docket entries after May 4, no news reports of plea negotiations, motions, or diplomatic deals that would force a schedule change

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will the June 30, 2026 hearing in U.S. v. Maduro Moros be rescheduled before May 31, 2026?

**Probability: 13% (YES)**

### Key Reasoning:

1. **Recently set by mutual agreement**: The hearing was scheduled just 16 days ago (May 4, 2026) after a joint letter from both defense and prosecution — the strongest indicator of stability.

2. **Short window**: Only ~11 days remain (May 20–31) for any rescheduling to happen and be reflected on the docket.

3. **Quiet phase**: The case is in a dormant pretrial period — the OFAC fees dispute was resolved, defense motions were withdrawn without prejudice, time has been excluded under the Speedy Trial Act, and no new motions are pending.

4. **No visible catalysts**: The most recent docket entries are from May 1–4, 2026. No news reports, filings, or public negotiations suggest an imminent schedule change.

5. **Tail risks acknowledged**: The ~13% accounts for genuine uncertainties — Judge Hellerstein's age (92), sealed filings not visible on public dockets, the Argentina extradition request, possible behind-the-scenes diplomatic developments, and the general unpredictability of this historically unprecedented case.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the June 30, 2026 hearing in U.S. v. Maduro Moros (SDNY) be rescheduled before May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43551)
[Prosecution of Nicolás Maduro and Cilia Flores - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prosecution_of_Nicol%C3%A1s_Maduro_and_Cilia_Flores)

[Federal Trial of Nicolás Maduro and Wife Cilia Flores Delayed Again in New York: When Is the Next Hearing?](https://www.latintimes.com/federal-trial-nicolas-maduro-wife-cilia-flores-delayed-again-new-york-when-next-hearing-597109)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, at any time on or before May 31, 2026 at 11:59 p.m. ET, the hearing currently scheduled for June 30, 2026 at 12:00 p.m. ET in United States v. Maduro Moros, et al., No. 1:11-cr-00205-AKH (S.D.N.Y.) is moved to a different calendar date (earlier or later) or vacated, as reflected on the public SDNY docket for case 1:11-cr-00205-AKH or as reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as Reuters, AP, Law360, or Courthouse News. The question resolves NO if the June 30, 2026 hearing remains scheduled on the docket for June 30, 2026 as of that resolution time.
fine_print: A change in the start time on June 30, 2026 alone (e.g., from noon to 2 p.m. on the same calendar date) does not count as a rescheduling; only a change to a different calendar date or vacatur of the hearing counts. A conversion from in-person to remote/video proceeding on the same date does not count. If the case is dismissed, transferred to another court, or the defendant pleads guilty before May 31, 2026 such that the June 30 hearing is removed or vacated from the docket, the question resolves YES. If Judge Hellerstein is replaced by another judge but the June 30 hearing date is maintained, the question resolves NO. If no public information is available by the resolution deadline as to the status of the June 30 hearing, the question resolves NO (i.e., the default assumption is that the hearing remains as scheduled).

factors.description: Hearing was set very recently (May 4) by mutual agreement of both parties - strong signal of stability
Only 11-day window (May 20-31) for rescheduling to occur - short timeframe limits probability
Case is in quiet pretrial phase - no pending motions, fees dispute resolved, motions withdrawn w/o prejudice
Prior rescheduling has occurred in this case (mid-March to March 26), showing it's not unprecedented
High-profile complex case with many moving parts (Argentina extradition, SAMs, multiple co-defendants) creates more opportunities for unexpected developments
Judge Hellerstein is 92 years old - health-related scheduling disruptions possible though unlikely in 11 days

input.anchor: Base rate for a recently-set federal hearing being rescheduled within an 11-day window when set by mutual agreement: ~15-20%. Federal criminal hearings get rescheduled ~20-30% of the time overall, but the short window and recent mutual agreement significantly reduce this.
input.assessment:
The June 30 hearing was set by the court just 16 days ago on May 4, 2026, after both the defense and prosecution filed a joint letter requesting the schedule. This is the strongest indicator of stability - both parties actively agreed to this date. The case is in a quiet phase: the major legal fees dispute that drove the prior rescheduling has been resolved, defense motions were withdrawn without prejudice, and time has been excluded under the Speedy Trial Act until June 30. 

For YES to occur, we'd need an unexpected development in just 11 days: a surprise plea deal, judicial health emergency, dramatic diplomatic development, or an urgent new motion. None of these have any visible precursors in the current docket or news coverage. The prior rescheduling (mid-March to March 26) was driven by the then-active fees dispute, which is no longer active.

The 15% estimate accounts for tail risks (judge health at age 92, surprise motions, unexpected diplomatic developments) while recognizing that the overwhelmingly likely outcome is that the hearing remains as scheduled through May 31.
input.calibration_notes: The 15% is somewhat above what a pure base-rate calculation might suggest for an 11-day window with no known catalysts, but accounts for the unusual complexity and profile of this case. I'm resisting the urge to push higher based on narrative reasoning about the case's complexity, since the specific question is about an 11-day window with a recently-set hearing date.
input.key_uncertainties: Main uncertainty is whether there are undisclosed negotiations or filings not yet on the public docket. Also uncertain about Judge Hellerstein's health status at 92. The Argentina extradition request adds a wild card but is unlikely to force action in 11 days.
input.tool_audit: Metaculus question fetch: successful, provided full question context. Research sub-agent: partially successful (one agent lacked web access, second provided comprehensive case background). Web search: successful across multiple queries. CourtListener docket fetch: very useful, confirmed latest entries through May 4. Latin Times article: useful for hearing history. No tool failures.
input.update_triggers: Any new docket entry in the case; news reports of a plea deal or diplomatic agreement; reports about Judge Hellerstein's health; emergency motion by either party.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was hit-or-miss - one sub-agent couldn't access web sources while the other could. The CourtListener docket fetch was the most valuable single source as it showed the actual latest filings. The question is relatively straightforward once you have the docket status - the key insight is that the hearing was set very recently by mutual agreement, which makes reschedule unlikely in 11 days.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Trace review — all factual claims verified:**
- **Factor 1** (hearing set May 4 by mutual agreement): Confirmed by CourtListener docket entry 309 showing May 4, 2026 memo endorsement, and Metaculus background confirming the April 24 joint letter.
- **Factor 2** (11-day window): Correct arithmetic from May 20 to May 31.
- **Factor 3** (quiet pretrial phase, motions withdrawn): Confirmed by docket entry 309 and Metaculus background.
- **Factor 4** (prior rescheduling mid-March to March 26): Confirmed by Latin Times article fetch showing "Second hearing: Originally scheduled March 17, delayed to March 26."
- **Factor 5** (complex high-profile case): Well-supported by extensive trace research on SAMs, Argentina extradition, multiple co-defendants.
- **Factor 6** (Judge Hellerstein age 92): Confirmed in Metaculus question text.
- **Factor 7** (no known catalysts): Supported by absence of post-May 4 docket entries and no news of rescheduling in web searches.

**No hallucinated evidence found.**

**Double-counting (mild):** Factors 1, 3, and 7 all draw on the same underlying evidence — the quiet, stable docket since May 4. However, the agent explicitly acknowledges this: "The factors may be over-counting correlated evidence (the recent setting, quiet phase, and no catalysts are related)." This self-awareness mitigates concern, and the factors do each add a slightly different dimension (recency/agreement, absence of disputes, absence of catalysts).

**Wrong-direction factors:** All logit signs match their descriptions. Negative logits push toward NO (stability), positive push toward YES (risk of change). ✅

**Resolution criteria:** Fully provided, fetched via Metaculus API, and correctly engaged. The agent understands the specific terms (calendar date change only, not time-only changes). ✅

**Probability assessment:** The factors sum to approximately -3.0 logits from a 50% prior, implying ~5%. The agent estimates 15%, a 10pp gap it explicitly acknowledges and explains by noting correlated downward factors and genuine tail risks in this extraordinary case. My independent estimate is ~8-12%, putting me slightly below the agent's 15% but not dramatically so. The direction is clearly correct (low probability), and the difference between 10-15% is within reasonable judgment for a question with real but hard-to-quantify tail risks (92-year-old judge, sealed filings, diplomatic dimensions). Not enough divergence for a fail.

**Anchor divergence:** Anchor is 15-20%, final is 15%. Well-aligned. ✅

**Counterargument quality:** Substantive — names specific mechanisms (Ker-Frisbie motions, sealed entries, diplomatic deals, judge health) and provides concrete probability-shifting thresholds. ✅

**Self-assessment:** 0.55 confidence is appropriate given solid case-specific evidence but inherent uncertainty in tail risk quantification. ✅

**Tool audit:** Agent claims "partially successful (one agent lacked web access)." Tool metrics show 0 errors across 13 calls. The "partial success" refers to the coherence_links sub-agent returning pre-2025 information (visible in the trace), which isn't a tool error per se but an information quality issue. Slightly imprecise but not materially misleading. ✅

**Worldview consistency:** Two Maduro research entries found in worldview store; both confirm the June 30 hearing as scheduled with no contradictions to the factors. No numeric/CDF sub-forecasts exist for cross-checking. ✅

**No past forecasts on similar hearing-rescheduling questions found in the forecast archive.**

Overall: Clean forecast with well-sourced factors, no fabricated evidence, correct engagement with resolution criteria, and a probability that is perhaps marginally high but within defensible range.
